**CISC 484: Introduction to Machine Learning** **Open Project — Phase 1 Proposal**

**Team Members:** Mizuho Okitani, Duc Duy Tran, Ryan Jones  
**Project Title:** Sentiment analysis on X users perception of Generative AI  
**Submission Date:** Mar 16

---

## **1\. Problem Definition & Scientific Hypothesis**

**1.1 Task Identification** 

This project focuses on a supervised multi-class text classification task in the domain of natural language processing (NLP). The objective is to classify tweets related to generative AI into three sentiment categories: **positive, neutral, and negative**. The classification will be performed using machine learning models trained on text representations derived from the tweet content. 

**1.2 Research Question & Hypothesis**

**Research Question:** Can transformer-based language models improve sentiment classification performance for generative AI discussions on X (Twitter) compared to classical machine learning models using TF-IDF features?

**Hypothesis 1:** We hypothesize that the DeBERTa-v3 transformer model will outperform classical machine learning models such as Logistic Regression and Linear SVM in sentiment classification accuracy and F1-score. This is because transformer-based models learn contextual embeddings, whereas classical models rely on TF-IDF representations that do not capture word order or contextual meaning.

**Hypothesis 2:** We also hypothesize that a transformer-based model will outperform baseline models in handling skewed categorical balanced dataset, because linear baseline models have bias towards the majority class, whereas in transformer-based model, it has a self-attention mechanism that assigns weight to the input sequence.   
---

## **2\. Dataset & Initial EDA**

**2.1 Dataset Description**

* **Dataset Name:** Generative AI tweets  
* **Source / URL:** [https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/arinjaypathak/generative-ai-tweets](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/arinjaypathak/generative-ai-tweets)   
* **Number of Instances:** 55596   
  **Number of Features:** 5  
* **Scale Requirement Met:** ◾️≥10,000 instances ☐ High-dimensionality

**2.2 EDA Visualizations**

**Plot 1 Type:** Daily Tweet Count about Generative AI



**Plot 2 Type:** Tweet Activity by Hour of Day 

**

**Plot 3 Type:** Top 20 Most Active User

**

**Plot 4 Type:** Top 20 Hashtags in Generative AI Tweet

**

**Plot 5 Type:** Distribution and Boxplot of Tweet Text Length

**

**2.3 Preprocessing Strategy**

Largely, we followed the preprocessing methods used in the related paper \[1\]. Preprocessing steps applied to the tweet text include converting text to lowercase and removing URLs, mentions, punctuation, numbers, special characters, extra whitespace, stop words, and hashtag symbols while retaining the words (e.g., \#AI → AI). After this processing, the cleaned text was stored in a new column called cleaned\_text and used to generate Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) \[2\] features for model training.

As a limitation of the related study, the skewed distribution of Negative/Positive labels was not addressed. In our study, we address this class imbalance by applying class\_weight="balanced", which automatically adjusts the weight of each class, and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) \[3\] to the TF-IDF feature matrix. SMOTE generates synthetic samples for the minority class by interpolating between existing minority-class instances.   
Since the dataset does not contain pre-labeled sentiment annotations, we will use TextBlob to automatically assign sentiment labels to each tweet. The related paper employed VADER \[4\]; however, based on a comparative study between VADER  and TextBlob \[5\], we selected TextBlob as our primary labeling tool, while retaining VADER as an alternative under consideration.

In order to answer our Hypothesis 2, we also want to keep the original set of labels for our baseline models and advanced model training in order to compare the performance of the advanced model without the advantage of having a more balanced dataset. 

---

## **3\. Comparative Methodology**

**3.1 Phase 1 Baseline Model**

Our baseline model will be Logistic Regression. After TF-IDF vectorization, the textual features in text-tweet become high-dimensional. Logistic Regression is suitable for classification tasks on our dataset.

Additionally, we will use Linear SVM as an alternative classical model as an experiment. Due to its efficiency with text classification, we want to see if the improvements of advanced models are consistent across different classical learning algorithms that are already best in slot for text classification tasks. 

**3.2 Phase 2 Advanced Architecture** 

In phase 2, we will implement a transformer-based deep learning model using DeBERTa-v3 model \[6\]. Unlike classical models that rely on TF-IDF vectorization, DeBERa uses contextual embeddings that separately models content and positional information within text — which we believe will allow the model to better capture semantic relationships and contextual nuances in short social media tweets. 

## **4\. Evaluation & Success Metrics**

**4.1 Primary Metrics**

| Metric | Why it fits this task |
| ----- | ----- |
| Macro F1-Score | The dataset is highly imbalanced, especially with very few negative tweets. Macro F1 evaluates performance across all classes equally, ensuring the model performs well on minority classes rather than the only majority class. |
| Precision & Recall | Precision helps measure how many predicted sentiments are correct, while recall evaluates how well the model detects each sentiment class. These metrics are especially important for the negative sentiment class, which is underrepresented in the dataset. |

**4.2 Validation Strategy** *(e.g., 5-fold cross-validation, stratified train/val/test split, temporal split)*

We will use a stratified train/validation/test split to preserve the distribution of sentiment labels across each subset of the dataset. The dataset will be divided into 70% training, 15% validation, 15% testing.

To ensure robust evaluation of the baseline models, we will also apply 5-fold stratified cross-validation during the training process. This allows the models to be evaluated on multiple splits of the dataset and reduces the risk of overfitting.

For each model, the final performance will be reported on the held-out test set, using accuracy, precision, recall, and macro F1-score. The best-performing baseline model will then be compared against the DeBERTa transformer model to determine whether the advanced architecture provides a significant improvement in sentiment classification performance.

---

*Due: March 15 · Submit as single PDF via Canvas · One submission per team*

---

## **Project Plan:**

###  **Phase 1**

- Preprocessing data (clean up tweet\_text column into cleaned\_text column)   
- Generates sentiment labels with TextBlob & VADER   
- Compare the generated labels margins between the 2 pattern analyzers: if the margin is too large, we need to find a different method of creating a supervised learning training data; if the margin is neglectable, we can keep the results of the labels generated by TextBlob.   
- TF-IDF vectorization & parameters setup using the cleaned\_text column  
- After we receive the vector matrix from TF-IDF, we will prepare 2 total training inputs:  
+ Input 1: Original sets of labels (which we know to be very imbalance; Negative 0.35%)  
+ Input 2: Processed sets of labels using SMOTE   
- Train baseline models: Logistic Regression & Linear SVM: 4 outputs (1)  
+ Logistic Regression with Input 1 (class\_weight="balanced")  
+ Logistic Regression with Input 2 (class\_weight="balanced")  
+ SVM with Input 1 (class\_weight="balanced")  
+ SVM with Input 2 (class\_weight="balanced")  
- Choose the best performance output of these 4 to be the baseline comparison metrics for phase 2

  
*Graph generated by Claude (Prompt: We wrote the project plan ourselves, then asked claude to visualize it in a chart)*

### **Phase 2:**

- Train advanced model: DeBERTa  
- Compare the result of the advanced model with the best performing output out of the 4  
- If DeBERTa outperformed the best output from (1), it supports both our hypothesis  
- If our Hypothesis 2 fails, we will validate Hypothesis 1 by transforming the training dataset to be a more balanced dataset by oversampling the negative tweets or weighted loss.

****  
*Graph generated by Claude (Prompt: We wrote the project plan ourselves, then asked claude to visualize it in a chart)*  
**References**  
\[1\] Ghotra, S. (2024). Public sentiment analysis on generative AI through machine learning. Zenodo. [https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18345286](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18345286)   
\[2\] Spärck Jones, K. (1972). A statistical interpretation of term specificity and its application in retrieval. Journal of Documentation, 28(1), 11–21. [https://doi.org/10.1108/eb026526](https://doi.org/10.1108/eb026526)  
\[3\] Chawla, N. V., Bowyer, K. W., Hall, L. O., & Kegelmeyer, W. P. (2002). SMOTE: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 16, 321–357. [https://doi.org/10.1613/jair.953](https://doi.org/10.1613/jair.953)   
\[4\] Hutto, C. J., & Gilbert, E. (2014). VADER: A parsimonious rule-based model for sentiment analysis of social media text. Proceedings of the International AAAI Conference on Web and Social Media, 8(1), 216–225. [https://doi.org/10.1609/icwsm.v8i1.14550](https://doi.org/10.1609/icwsm.v8i1.14550)   
\[5\] In, S., & Chanchamnan, S. (2026). Comparison of VADER and TextBlob labeling for sentiment analysis using machine learning and deep learning models: A study on generative AI user experience. Acta Psychologica, 253, 106268\. [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2026.106268](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2026.106268)   
\[6\] He, P., Gao, J., & Chen, W. (2021). Debertav3: Improving deberta using electra-style pre-training with gradient-disentangled embedding sharing. *arXiv preprint arXiv:2111.09543*.
